text: A data engineering team uses a stream to feed an ELT pipeline that merges changes into a target table. The source table receives INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations. The pipeline must capture all three operation types to correctly apply merges. Which stream configuration is appropriate?

options.A: An append-only stream, since it captures the net result of all changes
options.B: A standard stream with default settings
options.C: An insert-only stream configured on the source table
options.D: A standard stream with SHOW_INITIAL_ROWS = TRUE to include pre-existing data

explanation: Standard streams capture INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations via METADATA$ACTION and METADATA$ISUPDATE columns. Append-only streams only track inserts (ignoring deletes/updates). Insert-only streams are exclusively for external tables. SHOW_INITIAL_ROWS controls whether existing rows appear at stream creation — it does not affect which DML types are captured.

text: A developer clones a production database containing 500 tables. Immediately after the CLONE command completes, which statement about the cloned database is TRUE?

options.A: Storage costs double because each table's micro-partitions are physically copied
options.B: The clone contains references to the same micro-partitions and additional storage is consumed only as data diverges
options.C: Internal stages within the database are also cloned with their file contents
options.D: External tables in the source database appear in the clone with full data access

explanation: Snowflake cloning is zero-copy — the clone shares the original's micro-partitions via metadata pointers. New storage is consumed only when INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE modifies data in either copy. Internal stages are NOT cloneable (error). External tables are excluded from database clones.

text: A table has 10,000 micro-partitions. A query filters on column REGION, which is not part of the clustering key. The Query Profile shows 9,800 partitions scanned. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: The warehouse is undersized and cannot hold the partition metadata in SSD cache
options.B: The result cache has been invalidated by a recent DML operation on the table
options.C: The REGION column's values are distributed across most micro-partitions, causing overlapping min/max ranges
options.D: Snowflake only prunes micro-partitions when the filter column is the clustering key

explanation: Snowflake stores min/max statistics for ALL columns in each micro-partition, not just clustering key columns. However, if a column's values are spread randomly across partitions, the min/max ranges overlap extensively and almost no partitions can be eliminated. This is called poor natural clustering. Defining a clustering key on REGION would co-locate similar values and improve pruning.

text: An administrator runs SHOW VIEWS and can see the SQL definition of View_A but not View_B. Both views were created by the same role. What explains this difference?

options.A: View_B has a row access policy that hides its definition
options.B: View_B is a secure view, which hides the definition text from non-owner roles
options.C: View_B was created with TRANSIENT keyword, which obscures the definition
options.D: View_B references tables the administrator does not have SELECT on, so the definition is masked

explanation: Secure views hide the view definition (SQL text) and internal query optimizations from all roles except the view owner. Standard views expose the definition to anyone with SHOW VIEWS access. Row access policies filter rows, not view definitions. TRANSIENT is not a view keyword.

text: A warehouse has AUTO_SUSPEND = 0 and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. No queries have been submitted for 2 hours. What is the current state of the warehouse?

options.A: Suspended, because no queries were submitted for over 60 seconds
options.B: Running and consuming credits, because AUTO_SUSPEND = 0 means never auto-suspend
options.C: Suspended, because AUTO_SUSPEND = 0 means immediate suspension after each query
options.D: In a standby state that does not consume credits but maintains SSD cache

explanation: AUTO_SUSPEND = 0 means the warehouse NEVER automatically suspends — it runs continuously and consumes credits even with no activity. To enable immediate suspension, set AUTO_SUSPEND = 1 (minimum 60 seconds). There is no standby state in Snowflake.

text: An analyst queries SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY for a query that completed 30 minutes ago. The query does not appear in the results. The analyst then checks INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY and finds it immediately. What explains this?

options.A: The analyst lacks the required privileges to read ACCOUNT_USAGE views
options.B: ACCOUNT_USAGE views have a latency of up to 45 minutes, while INFORMATION_SCHEMA provides near-real-time results
options.C: ACCOUNT_USAGE only records queries that ran longer than 60 seconds
options.D: INFORMATION_SCHEMA retains queries for 365 days, providing a more complete history

explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE views have a latency of 45 minutes to 3 hours. INFORMATION_SCHEMA provides near-real-time data. However, INFORMATION_SCHEMA retains only 7-14 days of history (not 365 days — that is ACCOUNT_USAGE). ACCOUNT_USAGE records all queries regardless of duration.

text: A pipeline has three stages: Stage 1 joins orders with customers, Stage 2 aggregates by region, Stage 3 filters top regions and materializes output. Each stage must automatically refresh when upstream data changes. A colleague suggests using materialized views. Why is this approach problematic?

options.A: Materialized views cannot be queried by downstream materialized views
options.B: Materialized views do not support WHERE clauses
options.C: Materialized views are limited to single base table queries and cannot perform joins
options.D: Materialized views can only be refreshed manually using ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW REFRESH

explanation: Materialized views support only queries against a single base table — they cannot include joins, subqueries against other tables, or references to other MVs. Dynamic tables support complex queries including joins and can be chained into multi-stage pipelines with automatic refresh via TARGET_LAG.

text: Which of the following features is available in Snowflake Standard Edition?

options.A: Multi-cluster warehouses with auto-scaling
options.B: Dynamic data masking policies
options.C: Materialized views
options.D: Snowpipe continuous data loading

explanation: Snowpipe is available in all Snowflake editions. Multi-cluster warehouses, dynamic data masking, and materialized views all require Enterprise Edition or higher.

text: Dynamic table DT_A has TARGET_LAG = DOWNSTREAM. Dynamic table DT_B references DT_A and has TARGET_LAG = '15 minutes'. No other dynamic tables reference DT_A. If DT_A's source data changes, when does DT_A refresh?

options.A: DT_A refreshes every 15 minutes on a fixed schedule independent of DT_B
options.B: DT_A refreshes only when explicitly triggered by EXECUTE IMMEDIATE for DT_A
options.C: Snowflake schedules DT_A's refresh as needed so that DT_B can meet its 15-minute target lag
options.D: DT_A never refreshes because DOWNSTREAM requires at least two downstream consumers

explanation: TARGET_LAG = DOWNSTREAM delegates refresh scheduling to Snowflake. The system determines when to refresh DT_A based on the freshness requirements of its downstream consumers (in this case DT_B's 15-minute lag). DT_A does not have its own fixed schedule — Snowflake optimizes refresh timing to satisfy the pipeline.

text: A materialized view is defined on table ORDERS with a WHERE clause filtering status = 'SHIPPED'. A user queries SELECT * FROM ORDERS WHERE status = 'SHIPPED' AND region = 'US'. The user does not reference the materialized view. What happens?

options.A: The query scans the full ORDERS table because the MV was not referenced in the FROM clause
options.B: The query fails because materialized views require explicit references
options.C: The optimizer may transparently rewrite the query to read from the MV if it determines this is more efficient
options.D: The MV is used only if the query's WHERE clause exactly matches the MV's definition

explanation: Snowflake's optimizer can automatically rewrite queries against the base table to read from a materialized view when the MV can satisfy the query. The WHERE clause does not need to match exactly — the optimizer checks if the MV's precomputed results can be used to answer the query. This is fully transparent to the user.

text: An account-level network policy allows the range 10.0.0.0/8. A user-level network policy on user JSMITH allows only 192.168.1.0/24. User JSMITH connects from IP 10.0.5.100. What happens?

options.A: The connection succeeds because the IP is allowed by the account-level policy
options.B: The connection is blocked because the user-level policy overrides the account policy, and the IP is not in 192.168.1.0/24
options.C: Both policies are evaluated and the connection succeeds if either allows it
options.D: The connection fails with a policy conflict error

explanation: User-level network policies OVERRIDE account-level policies — they are not additive. When a user-level policy exists, ONLY that policy is evaluated. Since 10.0.5.100 is not in the user-level ALLOWED_IP_LIST of 192.168.1.0/24, the connection is blocked.

text: A developer creates a task without specifying a WAREHOUSE parameter. When attempting to resume the task, it fails with an insufficient privileges error. The role has CREATE TASK on the schema and EXECUTE TASK at the account level. What additional privilege is needed?

options.A: OPERATE on the task
options.B: EXECUTE MANAGED TASK at the account level
options.C: USAGE on a warehouse
options.D: MANAGE WAREHOUSES at the account level

explanation: Tasks without a WAREHOUSE parameter use serverless compute. Running serverless tasks requires EXECUTE MANAGED TASK at the account level, in addition to EXECUTE TASK. OPERATE is needed for resuming/suspending tasks the role owns but is separate from the compute privilege issue here.

text: A security administrator wants to ensure that every table created in ANALYTICS.REPORTING going forward automatically grants SELECT to the ANALYST role, without affecting existing tables. Which statement achieves this?

options.A: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS.REPORTING TO ROLE ANALYST
options.B: GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ANALYTICS.REPORTING TO ROLE ANALYST
options.C: ALTER SCHEMA ANALYTICS.REPORTING SET DEFAULT_GRANTS = 'SELECT TO ANALYST'
options.D: GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA ANALYTICS.REPORTING TO ROLE ANALYST

explanation: GRANT ... ON FUTURE TABLES applies only to tables created after the statement executes. ON ALL TABLES grants to existing tables. There is no DEFAULT_GRANTS schema property. GRANT ON SCHEMA grants schema-level usage, not table-level SELECT.

text: Table ownership is transferred from ROLE_A to ROLE_B using GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE t1 TO ROLE ROLE_B REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS. The ANALYST role previously had SELECT on t1. After the transfer, ANALYST queries t1 and gets an insufficient privileges error. What happened?

options.A: ROLE_B must explicitly grant USAGE on the table's schema to ANALYST
options.B: The REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS clause removed all existing privileges on the object, including ANALYST's SELECT grant
options.C: ANALYST must re-authenticate for the new ownership to take effect
options.D: Table ownership transfers are not allowed between custom roles

explanation: GRANT OWNERSHIP with REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS removes all existing outbound privileges on the object before transferring ownership. ANALYST's SELECT grant was revoked. To preserve existing grants during an ownership transfer, use COPY CURRENT GRANTS instead. If neither clause is specified and outbound grants exist, the command fails.

text: Which role can create new Snowflake accounts within an organization and enable replication between them?

options.A: ACCOUNTADMIN in any account within the organization
options.B: SECURITYADMIN with the MANAGE ACCOUNTS privilege
options.C: ORGADMIN
options.D: SYSADMIN with elevated organization privileges

explanation: ORGADMIN is the organization-level role that can create accounts, enable replication between accounts, and view organization-level usage. ACCOUNTADMIN operates within a single account. SECURITYADMIN and SYSADMIN do not have cross-account capabilities.

text: A provider creates a reader account for a partner who does not have Snowflake. The partner runs several analytical queries using the reader account's warehouse. Who is billed for the compute?

options.A: The partner, through a Snowflake pay-as-you-go arrangement
options.B: The provider, since all reader account costs are charged to the provider's account
options.C: Snowflake absorbs the cost as an incentive for data sharing adoption
options.D: The compute is free because reader accounts use a shared system warehouse

explanation: Reader accounts are created and fully managed by the provider. All compute and storage costs — including warehouse usage by the reader — are billed to the provider's Snowflake account. This is a critical cost consideration when offering reader accounts.

text: USERADMIN creates a new custom role DATA_LOADER and wants to grant it INSERT privileges on table ANALYTICS.RAW.EVENTS. Can USERADMIN perform this grant?

options.A: Yes, USERADMIN can create roles and grant any privilege on any object
options.B: No, USERADMIN can create roles and users but cannot grant object-level privileges like INSERT
options.C: Yes, but only if USERADMIN also owns the ANALYTICS database
options.D: No, only ACCOUNTADMIN can grant privileges on tables

explanation: USERADMIN has CREATE USER and CREATE ROLE privileges and can assign roles to users/other roles. However, granting object-level privileges (SELECT, INSERT, etc.) requires SECURITYADMIN (via MANAGE GRANTS) or the object owner. USERADMIN does not inherit MANAGE GRANTS.

text: Before loading 200 CSV files into a production table, an engineer wants to identify all rows across all files that would cause parsing errors, without inserting any data. Which approach is correct?

options.A: Run COPY INTO with ON_ERROR = CONTINUE and check VALIDATE() afterward
options.B: Run COPY INTO with VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ERRORS
options.C: Run COPY INTO with FORCE = TRUE and TRUNCATECOLUMNS = TRUE
options.D: Run COPY INTO with ON_ERROR = SKIP_FILE and review the error output

explanation: VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ERRORS validates all files and returns all error rows WITHOUT loading any data. ON_ERROR = CONTINUE actually loads data (skipping bad rows). FORCE bypasses dedup but still loads data. SKIP_FILE skips entire files with errors during actual loading.

text: An engineer loads Parquet files where the column names match the target table but in different order. The first load attempt fails with a type mismatch because columns were matched by ordinal position. Which COPY option fixes this?

options.A: ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH = FALSE
options.B: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME = CASE_INSENSITIVE
options.C: FORCE = TRUE
options.D: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE

explanation: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME loads columns by matching names rather than ordinal position. CASE_INSENSITIVE handles case differences between file and table column names. ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH handles count mismatches, not ordering. STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY is for JSON. FORCE bypasses load metadata tracking.

text: A VARIANT column stores JSON: {"customer": {"addresses": [{"city": "NYC"}, {"city": "LA"}]}}. An analyst needs each address as a separate row with the city value extracted. Which SQL pattern achieves this?

options.A: SELECT PARSE_JSON(data:customer:addresses) FROM t
options.B: SELECT f.value:city::STRING FROM t, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => t.data:customer.addresses) f
options.C: SELECT OBJECT_CONSTRUCT('city', data:customer:addresses) FROM t
options.D: SELECT ARRAY_TO_STRING(data:customer:addresses, ',') FROM t

explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN expands an array into separate rows. Each element becomes f.value, and f.value:city extracts the nested key. PARSE_JSON converts a string to VARIANT (the data is already VARIANT). OBJECT_CONSTRUCT builds new objects. ARRAY_TO_STRING concatenates array elements into a single string.

text: A COPY INTO load with ON_ERROR = CONTINUE and PURGE = TRUE processes 50 files. 3 files have scattered bad rows. After the command completes, how many files remain on the stage?

options.A: 50 — PURGE is ignored when ON_ERROR = CONTINUE
options.B: 3 — only the files with errors remain
options.C: 0 — all 50 files are purged because CONTINUE processes every file
options.D: 47 — only completely clean files are purged

explanation: With ON_ERROR = CONTINUE, Snowflake processes every file and loads all valid rows (skipping bad rows). PURGE = TRUE removes files that were processed — and since CONTINUE processes all files (including those with errors), all 50 files are purged. Files are only retained when SKIP_FILE causes them to be entirely skipped.

text: A data team loads files into a table using bulk COPY INTO. They attempt to reload the same files 60 days later without using FORCE = TRUE. The COPY command completes but reports 0 rows loaded. Ten days after that (day 70), they try again without FORCE. What happens?

options.A: 0 rows loaded — Snowflake's load metadata never expires
options.B: The files are loaded successfully because bulk COPY metadata is retained for 64 days and has now expired
options.C: The files are loaded successfully because bulk COPY metadata is retained for 14 days
options.D: The COPY command fails with a file-not-found error

explanation: Bulk COPY INTO retains load metadata for 64 days. Within that window, Snowflake skips files already loaded (dedup). At day 60, metadata exists so files are skipped. At day 70, metadata has expired (>64 days), so files are treated as new and loaded again. Snowpipe retention is 14 days, not bulk COPY.

text: A stream is created on table EVENTS. The table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 3 and MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14 (default). The stream goes unconsumed for 15 days. What is the stream's state?

options.A: Active — the 14-day extension parameter keeps the stream valid
options.B: Active — streams never go stale as long as the source table exists
options.C: Stale — 15 days exceeds the greater of DATA_RETENTION (3) and MAX_DATA_EXTENSION (14)
options.D: Stale — 15 days exceeds the DATA_RETENTION_TIME of 3 days

explanation: Stream staleness is determined by the GREATER of DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS and MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS. Here, max(3, 14) = 14 days. Since 15 > 14, the stream is stale and must be dropped and recreated. The retention value alone (3 days) would have made it stale much sooner, but the extension parameter gave it 14 days.

text: A data engineer needs to download files from an internal named stage to a local machine for analysis. The engineer attempts the operation from a Snowsight worksheet. What is the result?

options.A: The operation succeeds — Snowsight supports all stage operations
options.B: The operation fails — GET requires SnowSQL or a Snowflake driver with local filesystem access
options.C: The operation fails because GET only works with user stages, not named stages
options.D: The operation succeeds but files are downloaded to a temporary Snowflake-managed location

explanation: GET (and PUT) require a client with local filesystem access — SnowSQL CLI or Snowflake drivers (JDBC, Python connector, etc.). Snowsight runs in a browser and cannot access the local filesystem. GET works with all internal stage types (user, table, named).

text: Snowpipe loads files into a table continuously. The Snowpipe compute costs appear on the provider's bill. Which billing model describes how Snowpipe compute is charged?

options.A: Per-second billing using the virtual warehouse specified in the pipe definition
options.B: Per-second billing using Snowflake-managed serverless compute, with overhead charges for file management
options.C: Flat hourly rate regardless of data volume
options.D: No compute charge — Snowpipe only incurs storage costs for the stage

explanation: Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute (not user warehouses). It is billed per-second based on actual compute time, plus a small overhead for file queue management. There is no user-specified warehouse in the pipe — compute is fully managed.

text: A task is created with CREATE TASK my_task ... WAREHOUSE = my_wh AS ... A second task is created with CREATE TASK my_task2 ... AS ... (no WAREHOUSE specified). How do these tasks differ in their compute model?

options.A: Both use the same warehouse — the second task inherits the session's current warehouse
options.B: The first uses user-managed compute (specified warehouse); the second uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute
options.C: The second task fails at creation time because WAREHOUSE is required
options.D: The first uses dedicated compute; the second shares compute with other serverless tasks in the account

explanation: Specifying WAREHOUSE makes the task user-managed (billed to that warehouse). Omitting WAREHOUSE makes it serverless (Snowflake provisions and manages compute). Serverless tasks require EXECUTE MANAGED TASK privilege. The task creation succeeds in both cases.

text: A task DAG has root task T_ROOT with SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 * * * * UTC'. T_ROOT has two children: T_A (AFTER T_ROOT) and T_B (AFTER T_ROOT). T_A has child T_C (AFTER T_A). The root task's WHEN condition evaluates to FALSE at the scheduled time. What executes?

options.A: All tasks execute but return empty results
options.B: Only T_ROOT runs; children are suspended until the next schedule
options.C: No task in the DAG executes — the entire run is skipped
options.D: T_A, T_B, and T_C run because child tasks ignore the root's WHEN condition

explanation: When a root task's WHEN condition evaluates to FALSE, the entire DAG run is skipped — no compute is consumed by any task in the graph. The WHEN clause is evaluated before the task body executes. This is commonly used with SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA() to skip runs when no new data exists.

text: A multi-cluster warehouse uses SCALING_POLICY = 'STANDARD' with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4. During a brief spike, 20 queries queue simultaneously. How does the STANDARD policy respond compared to ECONOMY?

options.A: STANDARD and ECONOMY both wait 6 minutes before starting new clusters
options.B: STANDARD starts additional clusters immediately as queries queue; ECONOMY waits to confirm sustained load before adding clusters
options.C: STANDARD limits cluster additions to one per minute; ECONOMY adds all needed clusters instantly
options.D: STANDARD scales up cluster size; ECONOMY scales out cluster count

explanation: STANDARD scaling favors performance — it starts new clusters as soon as queries begin queuing. ECONOMY scaling conserves credits — it starts new clusters only when there is enough load to keep a cluster busy for at least 6 minutes. Both scale OUT (add clusters), not UP (resize).

text: A developer writes a UDF that needs to insert audit records into a logging table each time it is called. The UDF creation fails. Why?

options.A: UDFs cannot be written in SQL
options.B: UDFs cannot perform DML operations — they are limited to returning computed values without side effects
options.C: UDFs require a warehouse to be specified at creation time
options.D: UDFs cannot reference tables in other schemas

explanation: UDFs (user-defined functions) return scalar or tabular values and cannot execute side effects like INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or DDL. For operations requiring DML, use a stored procedure instead. Both UDFs and stored procedures support SQL, JavaScript, Python, Java, and Scala.

text: A developer needs to generate a sequence of 1,000 dates starting from '2025-01-01' without referencing any existing table. Which approach works in Snowflake?

options.A: SELECT GENERATE_SERIES('2025-01-01'::DATE, '2027-09-27'::DATE, INTERVAL '1 DAY')
options.B: SELECT DATEADD(DAY, SEQ4(), '2025-01-01'::DATE) FROM TABLE(GENERATOR(ROWCOUNT => 1000))
options.D: SELECT NEXT VALUE FOR my_sequence FROM DUAL

explanation: TABLE(GENERATOR(ROWCOUNT => n)) produces n rows without a base table. Combined with SEQ4() (auto-incrementing sequence function) and DATEADD, it generates date sequences. GENERATE_SERIES and RANGE are not Snowflake functions. DUAL is an Oracle concept not needed in Snowflake.

text: A query aggregates employee names per department: SELECT dept, ARRAY_AGG(name) FROM emp GROUP BY dept. What is the data type of the second column in the result?

options.A: VARCHAR — names are concatenated with commas
options.B: VARIANT — Snowflake wraps aggregated values in VARIANT
options.C: ARRAY — each group produces a Snowflake ARRAY of the aggregated values
options.D: OBJECT — each group produces a key-value map

explanation: ARRAY_AGG returns an ARRAY data type containing the aggregated values (e.g., ['Alice','Bob']). LISTAGG would return a delimited VARCHAR string. OBJECT_AGG returns an OBJECT (key-value pairs). The result is a native ARRAY, not VARIANT, though ARRAYs are stored in VARIANT internally.

text: A data team sets ALTER SESSION SET QUERY_TAG = 'etl_pipeline_v2' at the start of their loading session. Where does this tag appear?

options.A: As metadata attached to every table modified during the session
options.B: In QUERY_HISTORY views, labeling all queries executed in that session for filtering and cost attribution
options.C: As an object tag on the session's warehouse, visible via SHOW TAGS
options.D: In the RESULT_SCAN output of each query

explanation: QUERY_TAG is a session parameter that labels all queries in the session. The tag appears in QUERY_HISTORY (both INFORMATION_SCHEMA and ACCOUNT_USAGE) and is used for tracking, filtering, and cost attribution. It is not an object tag, does not attach to tables, and does not appear in RESULT_SCAN.

text: A table stores JSON events in a VARIANT column named RAW. To extract the event type from {"event": {"type": "click"}}, which expression is correct?

options.A: RAW.event.type::STRING
options.B: JSON_EXTRACT(RAW, '$.event.type')
options.C: RAW:event.type::STRING
options.D: GET_PATH(RAW, 'event/type')::STRING

explanation: Snowflake uses colon notation for VARIANT path traversal: column:key.subkey. RAW:event.type navigates to the nested 'type' key. The ::STRING cast converts the extracted VARIANT to a string. Dot notation without the leading colon (RAW.event.type) is invalid — the colon is required for the first key. JSON_EXTRACT is not a Snowflake function. GET_PATH uses dot separators, not slashes.

text: SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION on a table shows average_overlap = 12.5 and average_depth = 8.2. The clustering key is on column ORDER_DATE. What does this indicate about query performance on ORDER_DATE filters?

options.A: Excellent clustering — depth under 10 means most queries will be fully pruned
options.B: Poor clustering — high overlap and depth mean many micro-partitions have overlapping ORDER_DATE ranges, reducing pruning effectiveness
options.C: Normal state — all tables have depth between 5 and 15 by default
options.D: The table is too small for clustering metrics to be meaningful

explanation: Average overlap measures how many micro-partitions share overlapping value ranges for the clustering key. Average depth measures how many partitions a single value might span. Ideal values are close to 1. Values of 12.5 and 8.2 indicate significant degradation — reclustering (automatic or manual) may be needed.

text: A task has ALLOW_OVERLAPPING_EXECUTION = FALSE (default). The task is scheduled every 5 minutes but the previous run is still executing when the next scheduled time arrives. What happens?

options.A: The new run queues behind the active run and executes as soon as it finishes
options.B: The new scheduled run is skipped entirely
options.C: The active run is cancelled and the new run starts
options.D: Both runs execute concurrently on separate compute resources

explanation: With ALLOW_OVERLAPPING_EXECUTION = FALSE (the default), if the previous run is still active when the next scheduled time arrives, that run is skipped. It does not queue — it is simply omitted. Setting it to TRUE would allow concurrent execution.

text: A provider wants to share data with a consumer whose Snowflake account is in a different cloud region. The provider does not want to manually set up replication. Which sharing method supports this?

options.A: A direct share, since Snowflake handles cross-region sharing automatically for all share types
options.B: A listing with cross-region auto-fulfillment enabled
options.C: An external table pointing to the provider's cloud storage bucket in the other region
options.D: A reader account created in the consumer's region

explanation: Listings (private or Marketplace) support cross-cloud auto-fulfillment — Snowflake handles replication transparently. Direct shares are limited to consumers in the same Snowflake region. External tables require the consumer to have their own storage access. Reader accounts exist in the provider's region.

text: A database is created with CREATE DATABASE staging_db TRANSIENT = TRUE. Within this database, a developer runs CREATE TABLE staging_db.public.events (id INT, payload VARIANT) without specifying TRANSIENT. What Fail-safe period does the EVENTS table have?

options.A: 7 days — the standard Fail-safe for permanent tables
options.B: 0 days — tables in a transient database inherit the transient property regardless of CREATE TABLE syntax
options.C: 1 day — transient tables have a reduced Fail-safe period
options.D: 7 days, because omitting TRANSIENT from CREATE TABLE creates a permanent table

explanation: In a transient database, ALL objects inherit the transient property — even if CREATE TABLE does not specify TRANSIENT. Transient tables have 0 days of Fail-safe (and Time Travel limited to 0 or 1 day). The database-level transient setting cascades to all schemas and tables within it.

text: Table T1 has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 10. A clone T2 is created from T1. The engineer then runs ALTER TABLE T2 SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1. What is the Time Travel retention for T1 after this change?

options.A: 1 day — the ALTER on the clone also affects the source
options.B: 10 days — the source and clone have independent retention settings after cloning
options.C: 0 days — altering the clone's retention disables retention on the source
options.D: 5 days — Snowflake averages the retention between source and clone

explanation: Clones inherit the source's retention setting at clone time, but they are independent objects afterward. Altering the clone's DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS does not affect the source table. T1 remains at 10 days. T2 is now 1 day.

text: Which TWO objects can be included in a Snowflake direct share? (Select TWO)

options.A: Stored procedures
options.B: Secure views
options.C: Virtual warehouses
options.D: Secure UDFs

explanation: Direct shares can include tables, secure views, and secure UDFs. Stored procedures, virtual warehouses, tasks, stages, and non-secure views cannot be shared. Consumers use their own compute to query shared data.

text: A consumer creates a database from a direct share. The consumer attempts to run INSERT INTO shared_db.public.shared_table VALUES (1, 'test'). What is the result?

options.A: The INSERT succeeds because the consumer has full access to shared objects
options.B: The INSERT fails — shared databases are read-only; the consumer must CREATE TABLE AS SELECT to get a writable copy
options.C: The INSERT succeeds but is not visible to the provider
options.D: The INSERT fails because DML requires ACCOUNTADMIN role

explanation: Shared data is strictly read-only for consumers. No INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, or CLONE operations are allowed on shared objects. To obtain a writable copy, the consumer must create a local table using CREATE TABLE ... AS SELECT from the shared object.
